data.title: Dyadic outlines 2026 product revenue growth and commercialization milestones while expanding partnerships
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dyadic International, Inc. (DYAI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • President & COO Joseph Hazelton indicated that since stepping into the President's role in June 2025, the company has accelerated its transition "from a development stage platform company into a commercial product-driven biotechnology business with multiple paths for revenue." Hazelton highlighted significant progress, including a corporate rebrand, alignment around commercialization, technological advancements through CRISPR licensing, expanded manufacturing via the Fermbox partnership, and the initiation of product launches and distribution agreements.

  • Hazelton reported, "In less than one year, we've matured from early-stage product development to commercial product launches, distribution agreements, initial product sales and multiple revenue-generating partnerships."

  • Hazelton emphasized recombinant albumin as the leading example, noting the commercial launch with Proliant Health & Biologicals in early 2026. He described the arrangement as a profit-sharing collaboration, marking Dyadic's first platform-enabled product at commercial scale with recurring revenue potential.

  • Hazelton discussed other animal-free recombinant products, such as transferrin and DNase-1, and described an OEM distribution agreement with IBT Bioservices: "This accelerates market penetration while supporting both near-term revenue and positions us for long-term volume growth as products are adopted in customer workflows."

  • Hazelton highlighted the first sales of fibroblast growth factor (FGF) in Q4 2025, underscoring the milestone as both technical validation and initial revenue generation. He also detailed the food & nutrition segment, referencing a new agreement with BRIG BIO to develop and commercialize animal-free recombinant Bovine Alpha-Lactalbumin.

  • CEO Mark Emalfarb provided updates on biopharmaceutical collaborations, stating, "We continue to advance our partner-funded biopharmaceutical collaborations, applying the C1 platform into vaccines and antibody development through a nondilutive capital-efficient model."

  • CFO Ping Rawson stated, "For the year ended December 31, 2025, total revenue was $3.09 million compared to $3.5 million in 2024. The decrease was primarily driven by lower R&D collaboration activity and reduced the license and milestone revenue, partially offset by $1.86 million increase in grant revenue from the Gates Foundation and CEPI."

Outlook

  • Hazelton projected that as products move into routine use, the company expects "increasing repeat orders and revenue growth through 2026 and beyond."

  • Rawson stated that in 2026, Dyadic expects disciplined cash usage, prioritization of high-impact R&D programs, and anticipates growth in product revenues across life sciences and food and nutrition markets, "driven by new product launches in cell culture media while maintaining operating expenses generally in line with 2025 levels."

  • Rawson added, "Based on our current operating plan, we believe our existing cash resources provide runway into 2027. However, we will continue to evaluate additional capital resources, including strategic partnerships and capital market activities to further strengthen our balance sheet and support long-term growth."

Financial Results

  • Rawson reported total revenue of $3.09 million for 2025, with a primary driver being a $1.86 million increase in grant revenue, while R&D collaboration activity and license/milestone revenue declined. Cost of R&D revenue fell to $0.6 million, and G&A expenses decreased to $5.76 million. Internal R&D expenses rose modestly to $2.16 million. The net loss for 2025 was $7.36 million or $0.23 per share, compared to a net loss of $5.81 million or $0.20 per share in 2024. Cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash, and investment-grade securities at year-end totaled approximately $8.6 million. Net cash used in operating activities was approximately $5.7 million.

Q&A

  • Matthew Hewitt, Craig-Hallum Capital Group: Asked about the timing and ramp of product revenues for new launches through 2026 and beyond. Hazelton explained, "I would anticipate it's kind of a slower start. But as these companies get used to the products and as they get into the market and get established in the workflows, you can see that significantly start to pick up."

  • Hewitt also inquired about new collaborations and upfront license fees. Hazelton responded that it depends on the product and stage of development, stating, "Typically, we try to push for larger upfront access fees when the products are further along in their development phase."

  • John Vandermosten, Zacks Small-Cap Research: Asked about changes in internal management to handle expanded agreements. Hazelton described leveraging partner capabilities, saying, "All of our partners, we try to evaluate based on what else they can bring to the table and how quickly they can help us commercialize and accelerate these products."

  • Vandermosten followed up on pricing control. Hazelton clarified, "We have greater flexibility with products that we control. But obviously, in certain cases... they do tend to be market-driven, but the ones that we are able to control further are the ones that we have the greatest opportunity to improve our margins on."

  • Tony Bowers asked about the impact of higher energy costs and regulatory trends in food and biopharma. Hazelton pointed to regulatory scrutiny as a larger driver, while Emalfarb noted that alternative production methods are necessary for scale and affordability, referencing the company's technological edge.

  • Louis Titterton, private investor: Asked about breakeven timing. Hazelton could not provide a definitive answer but expressed confidence in the company's commercialization trajectory.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was generally inquisitive, focusing on product ramp timing, commercialization, pricing, and operational leverage. Questions probed for specifics on revenue timing, pricing flexibility, and breakeven, showing a neutral to slightly constructive outlook.

  • Management maintained a confident and strategic tone, repeatedly emphasizing the transition to commercialization and the scalability of the model. Phrases such as "the underlying business has clearly advanced towards commercialization" and "the foundation is now in place" signaled confidence.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained a similar tone, with analysts continuing to focus on execution and commercialization, and management emphasizing progress and strategic milestones. The tone remained constructive, with management providing more detail on commercialization in Q4.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Compared to Q3, the Q4 call emphasized concrete progress in commercialization, including product launches, distribution agreements, and initial product sales, while the previous quarter focused more on preparations and market positioning.

  • The strategic focus shifted from preparing and sampling in Q3 to active commercialization and revenue generation in Q4, with new agreements (e.g., BRIG BIO, IBT Bioservices) and the first sales of FGF.

  • Analysts' questions in both quarters centered on revenue ramp, operational scalability, and partnership leverage, but in Q4 there was increased inquiry into the practicalities of executing commercialization and market adoption.

  • Key metric changes included a decrease in total revenue year-over-year, driven by lower R&D and milestone revenue, partially offset by increased grant income. Operating losses widened, while cash usage and expense controls were highlighted.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the need to balance inventory production with market needs, the timing of market adoption, and the reliance on partners for sales and distribution as ongoing challenges.

  • Rawson addressed capital needs and financial flexibility, referencing the establishment of an ATM facility as a prudent step for opportunistic capital access. "The ATM simply provides optionality, and we will only access it if and when it makes sense."

  • Analysts raised concerns about the pace of revenue ramp, pricing flexibility, and the timing of breakeven, with management acknowledging uncertainties but expressing confidence in the path to recurring revenue.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored that Dyadic has transitioned from a development-focused organization to executing on commercialization, citing new product launches, expanded partnerships, and advancing biopharma collaborations as key drivers. The company projects growth in product revenue in 2026, supported by recurring streams from life sciences and food and nutrition markets, while maintaining disciplined cash management and leveraging partner capabilities to scale commercialization and support long-term value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Blue Owl, peers push back on private credit risks amid market jitters
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital (OWL) hasn’t seen a rise in defaults within its loan book, according to co-chief executive officer Doug Ostrover, who pushed back against growing concerns around private credit.

“We are not seeing an increase in defaults,” Ostrover said Thursday at the Asia Pacific Financial and Innovation Symposium in Melbourne. “We’re not seeing any signs of trouble,” he added, saying he remains “cautiously optimistic” about the near-term outlook.

“What we’re seeing in the portfolios today is pretty good growth, probably in the range of 8% to 10% revenue growth,” and a similar amount for earnings, Ostrover added.

Blue Owl (OWL) is among several private credit managers that have faced a wave of redemptions from retail-focused funds in recent months, partly tied to worries about software companies. Just this week, Ares Management (ARES) and Apollo Global Management (APO) capped withdrawals from certain funds amid rising redemption requests. Days earlier, Blackstone’s (BLK) flagship private credit fund reported its first monthly loss in over three years.

However, performance of portfolio companies appears solid, Ostrover stressed.

The comments echoed bullish sentiment for the $1.8T private credit market voiced by senior executives from Apollo (APO) and Blackstone (BLK) earlier in the day at the symposium.

“There is a big disconnect between the headlines and the news cycle and what we see in the portfolio,” Blackstone’s (BLK) co-chief investment officer Kenneth Caplan said at the symposium, adding the firm’s portfolio has “very low levels of default.”

Meanwhile, Apollo (APO) president Jim Zelter said “dramatic” headlines were creating opportunities and that there is no evidence of systemic risks in private credit.

Ostrover also mirrored the views of Zelter that distribution teams targeting retail and high-net-worth investors may not have clearly explained the liquidity restrictions inherent to the asset class.

The industry “could have done a better job” explaining these dynamics, Ostrover said. “Between us, and the advisers who sell our products, I don’t think we made it clear enough,” he said.

data.title: Ex-Goldman CEO Lloyd Blankfein warns Iran war fallout will persist
data.description:
• Goldman Sachs’ (GS) senior chairman and ex-CEO Lloyd Blankfein has warned that the damage from the Iran war “is going to last,” even if there were “a resolution tomorrow” - and urged investors to prioritize contingency planning amid the turmoil.
  • Speaking with CNBC’s Steve Sedgwick on Wednesday, Blankfein suggested that certain parts of the market may be too complacent in their approach to the conflict, adding that it’s equally dangerous to trade on the basis that “all will be resolved” as it is to say it will “never be resolved.”
  • “People know that, even if it stopped tomorrow, there’s so much damage to the infrastructure that the stress is going to last longer anyway, even if there was a resolution tomorrow, and there’s no reason to think there’s a resolution tomorrow,” he said of the Middle East war.
  • Blankfein pointed to the wild swings in energy markets (CL1:COM) (CO1:COM) in recent weeks as investors have sought to navigate the fallout from the conflict and price in the lasting impact from disrupted global oil supplies. Against this backdrop, he said investors should eschew conviction trades in favor of a more cautious approach, and “be very fleet of foot and very protective” of their positions.
  • He said the investment backdrop before the war in Iran was “more tailwinds than headwinds,” pointing to solid growth and a lower interest rate trajectory. “That’s all been made secondary or tertiary to what’s going on in the war and the price of energy,” he said.
  • Meanwhile, he said questions remain over the accuracy of valuation marks in private market funds’ portfolios, adding that assets have not been tested as equity markets have risen.

data.title: Health In Tech outlines $45M–$50M 2026 revenue target while advancing AI platform and large employer rollout
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Health In Tech, Inc. (HIT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Tim Johnson described 2025 as “a pivotal year for Health In Tech” and the company’s first as a public entity, highlighting a 71% revenue increase to $33.3 million and significant growth in distribution, platform expansion, and program innovation. Johnson emphasized, “Our business scales through distribution with brokers and TPAs serving as the primary channel,” noting the network expanded to 858 partners, up 34% year-over-year.
  • Johnson stressed the expansion of their Enhanced Do-it-Yourself Benefit Systems (eDIYBS) to support employers with over 100 employees, describing it as “a meaningful step-up market” and claiming the platform compresses underwriting timelines from roughly 3 months to about 2 weeks for larger employers.
  • Johnson addressed the company’s AI advantage, stating, “Our advantage is not just the AI model itself, it is the combination of proprietary data and integrated workflow and distribution.” He noted the system’s embedded, real-world data leads to ongoing improvements and increased platform stickiness.
  • The CEO detailed the three-year rate stabilization program, which he said “can deepen client relationships, improve retention and support expansion into larger employer segments, where budgeting stability is a critical decision factor.”
  • Johnson outlined 2026 strategic priorities, focusing on scaling the platform, expanding distribution, and investing in AI capabilities, “with the goal of evolving into a fully integrated marketplace that extends beyond underwriting to include claims administration, cost containment solutions and broader plan management capabilities.” He announced 2026 revenue guidance of $45 million to $50 million, representing approximately 35% to 50% year-over-year growth.
  • CFO Julia Qian stated, “For the full year 2025, the total revenue increased 71% year-over-year to $33.3 million. In the fourth quarter, the revenue increase to $53 million increased -- excuse me, increased to 53% to $7.5 million.” She highlighted operating leverage and ongoing investments in product and market development.

Outlook

  • Johnson projected full year 2026 revenue of $45 million to $50 million, noting, “Our confidence is supported by our ability to compress time to revenue, enabling new features to scale within 1 to 2 quarters compared to 12 to 24 months in traditional insurance environments.”
  • Qian reinforced, “We are providing full year 2026 revenue guidance of $45 million to $50 million, that representing about 35% to 50% growth year-over-year.”
  • Management cited continued investment in platform and AI capabilities, expansion of distribution, and beta testing of new solutions integrating physiological and claims data as key growth drivers.

Financial Results

  • Qian reported, “Adjusted EBITDA for the full year was $4.1 million, which is about 12.3% of the revenue, increased 81% year-over-year. Net income, adjusted EBITDA most comparable GAAP measure for the full year was $1.2 million, representing about 4% of the revenue, increased 91% year-over-year.”
  • For Q4, adjusted EBITDA was $0.3 million compared to $0.5 million in the prior year, and net income for the quarter was negative $0.3 million versus negative $0.1 million previously, reflecting reinvestment and seasonal enrollment dynamics.
  • The company generated $3.1 million in positive operating cash flow for 2025 and ended the year with $7.7 million in cash and cash equivalents.
  • Operating expenses for the year were $19.4 million, representing 58% of revenue, with sales and marketing expenses at $4.2 million and R&D investment including $3.2 million in capitalized software development.

Q&A

  • Allen Klee, Maxim Group: Asked about feedback on the large employer offering and three-year rate stabilization product. Qian explained financial impact is minimal so far as the official launch was in September, but “you will see more benefits in 2026.” Johnson noted the product launch is scheduled for next month and the platform is “really helpful for anybody that uses it.”
  • Klee inquired about the timing of renewals and potential for new business. Qian clarified that most business is currently in the small- to mid-sized group, and large group renewals will be new customer acquisitions, with January cited as the largest effective date for municipalities.
  • Klee asked about beta testing of physiological and claims data integration. Qian described the initiative as enabling “additional insights of the correlation, their health condition versus their medical condition.”
  • Marla Marin, Zacks: Queried differences in retention for large organizations. Johnson emphasized, “The stickiness will come because of the ease of use of the system, the tool dibs, it is extremely easy and efficient.”
  • Marin asked about expanding partnerships and future affiliations. Johnson responded that the tool “really expanded who we traditionally thought our market was,” and Qian added, “we have multiple different legs to grow for the company.”
  • Klee sought clarification on the cost containment and claims strategy. Qian outlined that “the marketplace function expanded...not just purchase the product. They also want to use the functionality doing their underwriting.”
  • Klee followed up on a partnership on the prescription side. Johnson stated, “They're a TPA, but they just happened to be owned to buy a PBM. So it's just another distribution source for us,” adding prescription management is not a current area of focus.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive but probing tone, focusing on the ramp of new offerings, renewal cycles, and broader market adoption, as evidenced by Klee’s and Marin’s detailed questions about timing and product traction.
  • Management demonstrated confidence in both prepared remarks and responses, using phrases like “we are providing full year 2026 revenue guidance” and “we believe this offering can deepen client relationships,” while occasionally deferring on specifics regarding new product beta results or pricing details.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained optimistic and forward-looking, with greater specificity on near-term product launches and distribution expansion. Analyst tone was consistent, seeking more clarity on timing and market impact.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • 2025 Q4 saw the formal launch of the large employer platform and the rollout of the three-year rate stabilization program, with more explicit 2026 revenue targets ($45 million to $50 million) and a stronger push on AI-driven features.
  • The previous quarter emphasized the debut of large employer capabilities, the HITChain blockchain initiative, and early pilot testing of the rate hold program, with a focus on upcoming Davos events and broader technology innovation.
  • Management in Q4 provided more concrete product feedback and outlined a clear timeline for new initiatives. Analyst focus shifted toward near-term revenue realization and the impact of new programs on customer growth and retention.
  • Financial guidance language became more specific in Q4, with a detailed revenue range and commentary on compressed time-to-revenue for new features.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that employer decision cycles may create quarter-to-quarter revenue variance, emphasizing that “year-over-year performance is a more meaningful way to evaluate the business.”
  • Qian cited planned reinvestment in go-to-market initiatives and product launches as reasons for lower Q4 net income, indicating a willingness to accept near-term profit pressure in pursuit of longer-term growth.
  • Analyst questions highlighted the long sales cycles and the nascent adoption of large group and three-year rate stabilization programs, suggesting possible delays in full revenue impact.
  • Prescription management remains an area of uncertainty given regulatory flux, with Johnson stating, “We don't want to commit to anything and then have something taken away from us.”

Final Takeaway

Health In Tech’s management delivered a message of strong growth and expanding opportunity, underscored by a 71% revenue increase in 2025 and a 2026 revenue target of $45 million to $50 million. The company is scaling its distribution network, investing in proprietary AI-driven platform advancements, and launching new offerings for larger employers and multi-year rate stabilization. Management acknowledged timing uncertainties and ongoing investments but expressed confidence in the durability of the business model, the compounding advantage of integrated workflow and data, and a significant long-term market opportunity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hyundai Motor aims to more than double China sales, launch 36 new models by 2030 in North America
data.description:
• Hyundai Motor (HYMLF) on Thursday announced plans to more than double its sales in China over the medium term and introduce 36 new models in North America by 2030.
  • At ​its annual shareholder meeting, Hyundai Motor CEO ​Jose Munoz said the South Korean automaker aims to sell 500,000 vehicles annually in China as ​part of its medium-term efforts, which ​would be more than double its current volumes, Reuters reported.
  • In North ‌America, ⁠Hyundai Motor said it plans to introduce 36 new models by 2030 in what it described as its most profitable ​region.
  • The figure includes ​variants ⁠of each vehicle model, such as electric, hybrid and gasoline-powered versions, the report said. ​Hyundai currently sells 25 models in ​North ⁠America, which includes the U.S. market with 20 models.
  • Shares of Hyundai Motor (HYMLF) were trading down 2.4% ⁠at press time, compared with ​the benchmark KOSPI index's (KOSPI) 3.1% drop.

data.title: Archrock announces planned retirement of CFO Douglas Aron by end of 2026
data.description:
Archrock (AROC) announced that Douglas S. Aron, the Senior Vice President and CFO, plans to retire by the end of 2026 or once a successor is appointed.

The company has hired an executive search firm to help find and evaluate potential replacements.

President and CEO Brad Childers praised Doug for his significant contributions and leadership during a time of transformation and improved financial performance at Archrock.

data.title: Asia equities retreat after Iran rejects Trump's 15-point truce plan
data.description:
Asian equity markets declined on Thursday, ending a two-day rise due to conflicting news about peace talks between the US and Iran. While the US claimed negotiations were continuing, Iran rejected the idea of direct talks and a US ceasefire, proposing its plan instead. 

WTI crude futures rose above $92 per barrel on Thursday, recovering part of the previous session’s losses.

Gold prices fell below $4,500 per ounce on Thursday, giving back gains from the past two sessions.

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 0.79% to below 53,600, while the broader Topix Index declined 0.5% to 3,630 on Thursday, ending a two-day rebound. The Japanese yen fell to around 159.5 per dollar on Thursday, marking a third consecutive session of losses.

China (SHCOMP) fell 0.88% to 3,917, while the Shenzhen Component Index dropped 0.3% to 13,756 on Thursday, and the offshore yuan steadied around 6.90 per dollar, following two sessions of losses.

Meanwhile, the White House said US President Donald Trump is set to visit Beijing on May 14–15 for a high-level summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping.

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 2.01% to around 25,071 on Thursday, extending losses.

India (SENSEX) markets closed.

India has reaffirmed its inflation-targeting framework, setting a 4% retail inflation goal with a tolerance band of 2–6% for the five years from April 2026 to March 2031. 

Australia (AS51) fell 0.10%, hovering near 8,524 after two sessions of gains, as traders weighed caution from the central bank. The Australian dollar traded below $0.695, holding steady at a seven-week low.

The Reserve Bank of Australia warned that the war in Iran may cause a global supply shock, leading to increased inflation and long-term expectations. This situation could raise short-run neutral rates and require stricter policy.

In the U.S. on Wednesday, all three major indexes ended higher as emerging reports of a 15-point US peace proposal delivered to Tehran fueled hopes for a diplomatic end to the Middle East conflict.

U.S. stock futures drifted flat to lower on Thursday as investors continued to monitor developments in the Middle East amid heightened uncertainty over efforts to end the Iran war: Dow -0.37%; S&P 500 -0.34%; Nasdaq -0.43%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia's markets and economy:

data.title: Navan expects 24% revenue growth for 2027 while advancing AI-driven travel offerings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Navan, Inc. (NAVN) Q4 2026

Management View

  • Ariel Cohen, Co-Founder, CEO & Chairman, highlighted "we are doing it. We just closed a very good Q4 and a year with incredible results. Our NPS in Q4 is at 47%. This is all-time high. Our CSAT is at 96% and maintained very high."
  • Cohen pointed to "35% Q4 year-over-year revenue growth and the non-GAAP operating profit in the quarter" as demonstration of strong execution, adding "we signed net new GBV that is over 50% more compared to the Q4 in the previous year."
  • Cohen announced "we actually turned free cash flow positive for the first time in our history and a year ahead of our plan."
  • Cohen described the launch of Navan Edge, the company’s new AI-driven executive travel assistant, targeting a $57 billion market.
  • Cohen said, "we also recently announced the migration of the Reed & Mackay customers to our AI platform."
  • For fiscal 2027, Cohen stated the company will focus on "high growth, scaling in all channels and with all of our offerings, accelerating our innovation, which means that we will continue to invest in AI and to release new products and capabilities using our AI platform."
  • Aurelien Nolf, Chief Financial Officer, stated "Q4 revenue was $178 million, up 35% year-over-year, while our GBV reached $2.3 billion, up 42% year-over-year, a growth acceleration driven by an incredible go-to-market momentum and faster-than-expected enterprise onboarding and ramp."
  • Nolf added, "Our non-GAAP operating margin was breakeven, a remarkable 1,100 basis points improvement over last year."
  • Nolf addressed the $36.2 million noncash amortization charge due to retiring the Reed & Mackay brand for new sales, describing it as a "strategic onetime move."
  • Nolf reported, "We ended the year with a very strong balance sheet, $741 million in cash and short-term investments against just $125 million in debt."

Outlook

  • Nolf provided guidance: "for the full year 2027, we expect revenue between $866 million and $874 million or 24% growth at the midpoint and a non-GAAP operating profit between $58 million and $62 million, a 7% margin at the midpoint."
  • For Q1 fiscal 2027, "we expect revenue between $204 million and $206 million, which represents 30% growth as we head into a seasonally strong spring with non-GAAP operating profit expected to be in the range of $4.5 million to $5.5 million."
  • Management described the guidance as prudent, with Cohen stating, "it's very, very early days in the year."

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue was $178 million, up 35% year-over-year.
  • Gross booking volume (GBV) was $2.3 billion, up 42% year-over-year.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin was breakeven, improving by 1,100 basis points over last year.
  • GAAP operating margin was negative 50% in Q4, primarily due to the $36.2 million noncash amortization charge.
  • Free cash flow turned positive for the first time, a year ahead of plan.
  • The company ended the year with $741 million in cash and short-term investments and $125 million in debt.

Q&A

  • Steven Enders, Citigroup: Asked about bookings momentum and sustainability of growth. Nolf clarified "the 50% Ariel mentioned is the new signed GBV... the total annual travel spend that we expect from new customers that we just signed during the quarter."
  • Enders asked about impact from geopolitical conflict. Nolf responded, "we're seeing very minimal impact... low single-digit volume exposed to the Middle East."
  • Samad Samana, Jefferies: Asked about the Reed & Mackay transition. Cohen explained, "the main reason for accelerating the integration... is that, that's what our customers want" and highlighted improvements in unit economics and gross margins.
  • Samana requested color on GMV growth versus usage yield. Cohen replied, "I'm expecting bookings to grow slightly faster than revenue... may see a 30 basis point yield change in 2027 versus what we see in 2026."
  • Gabriela Borges, Goldman Sachs: Questioned payments strategy amid expense control. Cohen said, "we are growing the payment business, right? In fact, we plus 19% year-over-year in Q4."
  • Sitikantha Panigrahi, Mizuho: Asked about guidance factors and industry tailwinds. Nolf stated, "we are not seeing any impact from any geopolitical tensions right now in our business."
  • Scott Berg, Needham: Queried economics of new restaurant bookings. Nolf stated, "the managed is not a significant contributor to our 24% year-over-year revenue guidance, right? We are -- it's early days."
  • Jed Kelly, Oppenheimer: Asked about NDC and corporate travel. Cohen stated, "Navan is the leader in that, which means that we connect to airlines sometimes actually a lot of -- in a lot of the cases directly through the NDC protocol."
  • Patrick Walravens, Citizens JMP: Queried RFP volumes. Michael Sindicich, President, responded, "there's a lot of consolidation in the space... there's a lot of turmoil, which we're steady. We're growing fast."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on sustainability of growth, impact of macro factors, and financial integration, with a generally positive yet probing tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat stance in prepared remarks, with Cohen stating "we are doing it" and Nolf describing "incredible momentum."
  • During Q&A, management provided detailed clarifications, occasionally using phrases like "we believe" and "it's very, very early days," indicating prudent optimism.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management was more assertive about AI-driven differentiation and financial discipline, while analysts retained a constructive but cautious approach.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for FY2027 is now explicit, targeting 24% revenue growth and 7% non-GAAP operating margin at the midpoint, compared to the previous quarter where guidance for FY2027 was deferred.
  • Management emphasized free cash flow positivity a year ahead of plan, a new milestone not mentioned in the previous quarter.
  • There was a stronger focus on AI platform advances, notably the launch of Navan Edge and the integration of Reed & Mackay customers.
  • Analyst questions shifted toward integration execution, payments growth, and new product economics, reflecting evolving priorities.
  • Management tone in Q4 was more self-assured, highlighting operational achievements and future ambitions, whereas the previous quarter’s tone was more focused on establishing credibility following the IPO.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited minimal direct impact from geopolitical disruptions, with Nolf explaining "very, very low volume exposed to the Middle East."
  • Guidance assumes "a typical amount of disruption we are expecting to see in the world."
  • Integration of Reed & Mackay presents risks but is positioned as a strategic move to improve gross margin and customer experience.
  • Analysts probed on yield dynamics, segment mix, and sustainability of recent growth trends.

Final Takeaway

Navan, Inc. closed fiscal 2026 with record customer satisfaction and a significant shift to operational profitability, underpinned by rapid adoption of its AI-driven travel solutions and the successful integration of Reed & Mackay onto its platform. Management projects robust growth into fiscal 2027, with a focus on accelerating innovation, scaling across customer segments, and enhancing financial discipline, while maintaining confidence in its ability to capitalize on industry consolidation and technological differentiation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: FiscalNote shares to be delisted from NYSE; stock falls
data.description:
• FiscalNote Holdings (NOTE) has received notification from the New York Stock Exchange that it no longer complies with the NYSE’s continued listing standard requiring companies to maintain an average closing share price of at least $1.00 over a consecutive 30 trading-day period.
  • The company's shares fell 4% in extended trading following the announcement, it is down 64% YTD.
  • Accordingly, the NYSE has determined to commence delisting proceedings pursuant to Section 804 of the NYSE Listed Company Manual.
  • Trading in the Class A common stock and warrants on the NYSE will be suspended immediately, and the company expects trading to commence on the OTC markets on March 26, 2026, under the same ticker symbol.
  • The company is evaluating alternatives to appeal the delisting determination to the exchange's listing qualifications panel, although there can be no assurance that any such appeal, if made, will be successful, it said.

data.title: KKR’s $4.75B CoolIT sale triggers massive payouts: Employees to receive up to 8 years of pay
